headline: Ukraine's Drones Halt Russia's Biggest Refinery
deck: A strike some 2,500 kilometres into Siberia stopped crude processing at Omsk, Russia's top gasoline plant, while at the Ankara summit Trump told Zelensky that Washington will license Kyiv to build its own Patriot interceptors. Both moves point one way: Kyiv is trying to out-reach Russia's fuel and out-last its missiles.

art.title: The Reach: Ukraine to Omsk
art.caption: Ukrainian drones halted Russia's largest refinery at Omsk, roughly 2,500 km from the border, one of the deepest strikes of the war · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: Russia's largest oil refinery stopped processing crude on 7 July after Ukrainian drones struck its main distillation unit. The Omsk plant is the country's top gasoline producer, and its CDU-10 unit, which the strike set on fire, accounts for roughly 38 percent of the site's capacity; fuel shortages are expected to widen across Russia as a result [E1]. The refinery sits on the northern edge of Omsk in western Siberia, around 2,500 kilometres from the Ukrainian border, and Ukraine's General Staff called the hit one of the deepest attacks on Russian territory since the full-scale invasion [E2].

The damage was industrial, not human. Anatoly Seryshev, President Putin's envoy to Siberia, said facilities at the Gazprom Neft-operated refinery were damaged but that no plant personnel were injured [E1]. That distinction is the point of the campaign: Kyiv is not trying to kill refinery workers 2,500 kilometres away, it is trying to take a distillation column offline and let the fuel math do the rest [E1][E2].

The second move landed the same week, at the NATO summit in Ankara. Meeting Zelensky, Trump said the United States would grant Ukraine a licence to manufacture Patriot missile interceptors, telling him, "We're going to give a license to you to make Patriots" [E3]. He paired that with a limit: Washington would not hand over more of its own Patriot systems because the US needs them, and he acknowledged he had not yet told the manufacturers, Lockheed Martin and RTX, of the decision [E3].

Read together, the refinery and the licence describe one strategy from two ends. Ukraine is reaching deep to drain the fuel and logistics that move Russian forces, and it is trying to secure a domestic supply of the only interceptor that reliably stops Russian ballistic missiles, as its imported stocks run low [E1][E3]. Production and range, not a single spectacular hit, are the war Kyiv is now fighting.

The claims here are bounded. A halted distillation unit is a supply shock, not a fuel collapse, and Russia has restarted damaged units before [E1]. A verbal licence is not a factory: no co-production terms, timeline, or manufacturer sign-off have been disclosed [E3]. The discriminators are concrete and near-term, whether Omsk resumes full processing, and whether the Patriot pledge turns into an actual line with parts and dates. Until then, the reach is real and the interceptors are still a promise [E1][E3].

evidence_box.fragment: Omsk refinery halted operations after a Ukrainian drone strike damaged the CDU-10 unit; fuel shortages expected; no personnel injured
Gazprom Neft refinery on the outskirts of Omsk, around 2,500 kilometres from the border with Ukraine; one of the deepest attacks on Russian territory
Trump says US will grant Ukraine a license to build Patriot interceptors: "We're going to give a license to you to make Patriots"; manufacturers not yet informed
